content:
Ryan, that move—turning “the windows of heaven were opened” into “a meteorological condensation event”—seems small, even reasonable. But over time it trains the soul to live in a thinner world.

What you gain is a description of mechanism. What you lose is the register of meaning: agency, address, wonder, judgment, mercy, and gift. Scripture isn’t trying to furnish a lab report; it’s telling you what God did. “Windows of heaven” is ancient, poetic, and true: it says with force that God himself acted, lavishly and decisively. Reduce that to impersonal process and the following shifts slowly take place.

- God’s agency gets sidelined. You start to see only secondary causes and forget the Primary Cause. Jesus says the Father “sends rain on the just and the unjust.” James says Elijah prayed and it rained. If rain is only condensation, prayer becomes pious chatter rather than real pleading to a living Lord who governs means and ends.

- Awe drains away. Poetry is how Scripture tutors worship. Flatten the image and you thin out doxology. The heart that can only say “hydrological cycle” finds it harder to say “Bless the Lord, O my soul.”

- The world becomes impersonal. Gifts become “outcomes.” Gratitude erodes. Instead of receiving, you manage. Over time that produces pride when things go “as expected” and despair when they do not.

- You stop being addressed. The text now passes through your filter to be made respectable to modern ears, so you stand over it rather than under it. That posture makes it easier, next time, to sand off whatever else feels too sharp, until Scripture can no longer confront or comfort you.

- Meaning flattens to data. “Windows of heaven” carries moral and eschatological weight—judgment, cleansing, new creation (think Genesis 7–9; Malachi 3). “Condensation event” cannot carry that freight, so the moral imagination starves.

- Community worship thins. In corporate prayer and song, you need a shared world where God actually acts. If your inner translation always disenchants the text, you will feel out of step with the church’s praise.

Now, the opposite mistake is wooden literalism—insisting there are physical panes and hinges in the sky. Scripture speaks truly in human language, images, and phenomenological description. Inerrancy doesn’t demand we deny secondary causes. It calls us to receive what God is actually saying through the words he chose. He “opens the windows” is his way of asserting personal, sovereign action through the very processes we can measure.

The better way is to keep both registers in their place:
- In the lab, speak of pressure systems and condensation with gratitude for a world that is intelligible because the Word upholds it.
- In the sanctuary (and your heart), let Scripture’s speech govern. Say with the psalmist, “He waters the earth,” and mean it. Name the Giver, not just the gift’s mechanics.

Practices that help re-train the soul:
- Read the Psalms aloud. Let the metaphors stand without immediate paraphrase.
- When it rains, thank your Father by name. When it doesn’t, ask him. He delights to work through ordinary means.
- Ask of every passage: What is God doing? What is he revealing of himself? What response—faith, repentance, hope—is he seeking from me?
- Refuse the reflex to translate living words into clinical ones. The thinner language can be useful, but don’t let it swallow the thicker.

In short, that “translation” costs a man the ability to live in a God-breathed world. He keeps the barometer and loses the blessing. Far better to let the windows be windows—signs that the Lord of heaven opens and closes his storehouses—and then, if you like, go check the forecast with a thankful heart.
